action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: king, bird, hunter, farmer, dog, hill, spear, lake, road, boat, tree, stone, river, ship
Adjectives: big, red, cold, blue, small, tall, old, dark
Verbs (write them in this base form): see, chase, feed, find, watch, hold, carry, hear
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a king see a bird
    six kings see a bird
    a big king see a bird

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    the five lakes carry the big farmer  =>  gugepa fugo fogupo getupopa ti
    five hunters watch the two red ships  =>  gisapopa fago te kutopi sukupo ti
    a blue road feed the five farmers  =>  gugepopa ti totu tigo lofa
    a cold road find three ships  =>  gisapo pi kutofo tigo gugefe
    the four hunters find the three big rivers  =>  gesufapopa fugo pi kutofo sukupopa se
    the road find three red roads  =>  tigopo fago pi kutofo tigopa
    the two boats chase the four kings  =>  logipopa se tuli gasitipopa te
    three stones find the two old trees  =>  palepopa giso te kutofo gulupo pi
    three farmers watch the six rivers  =>  gesufapopa pufa kutopi gugepo pi
    the two rivers watch a tall hill  =>  kugi gole kutopi gesufapopa te
    the two hills feed three dark dogs  =>  fapopo lulo pi totu kugipopa te
    the five birds carry six rivers  =>  gesufapo pufa fogupo kafepopa ti
    the four dark boats hold four dark spears  =>  kasefupo lulo se gile gasitipopa lulo se
    the two lakes hear a dark hill  =>  kugi lulo fosu getupopa te
    two small birds hear a dog  =>  fapo fosu kafepo lesa te
    a hill see a tall river  =>  gesufa gole kake kugi

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to figure out the basic word order and how grammatical categories are marked. Let me start by testing simple sentences to understand: 1) word order (SVO?), 2) how articles work, 3) how numbers work, 4) how adjectives relate to nouns, and 5) what the verb forms are. I'll test with simple combinations.

PROBES:
1. a king see a bird
2. the king see a bird
3. a king see two birds
4. two kings see a bird
5. a king see two red birds
action.label: response

action.content: at most 4 probes per round, but 5 were given

Your last response could not be processed: at most 4 probes per round, but 5 were given.
List your probes (a brief THOUGHTS line is optional):
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence>
(up to 4 probe lines; at least one)
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to reduce to 4 probes. Let me test the most basic patterns: word order, articles, numbers, and adjectives.

PROBES:
1. a king see a bird
2. the king see a bird
3. a king see two birds
4. two kings see a bird
action.label: response

probes: a king see a bird
the king see a bird
a king see two birds
two kings see a bird

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a king see a bird"  =>  kafe kake logi
2. "the king see a bird"  =>  kafe kake logipa
3. "a king see two birds"  =>  kafepo te kake logi
4. "two kings see a bird"  =>  kafe kake logipo te
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 5 done. Probes used: 4 of 20. 4 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the results, I can see:
- Word order: Subject (noun phrase) + verb + object (noun phrase)
- "a" = indefinite singular, "the" = definite singular
- "two" appears to be "te" (indefinite plural marker?)
- "logi" = bird, "logipa" = the bird (definite), "logipo" = birds (plural)
- "kafe" = king, "kafe kake" = king (with adjective?)
- "kake" seems to be an adjective meaning "tall" or similar

Let me test more to understand: how adjectives work, how verbs work, and test other nouns.

PROBES:
1. a king see a bird
2. a big king see a bird
3. a king chase a bird
4. a bird chase a king
action.label: response

probes: a king see a bird
a big king see a bird
a king chase a bird
a bird chase a king

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a king see a bird"  =>  kafe kake logi
2. "a big king see a bird"  =>  kafe kake logi fugo
3. "a king chase a bird"  =>  kafe tuli logi
4. "a bird chase a king"  =>  logi tuli kafe
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 5 done. Probes used: 8 of 20. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the results:
- "fugo" = big (adjective)
- "tuli" = chase (verb)
- Word order: Subject + verb + object
- "kafe" = king, "logi" = bird
- "kafe kake" = king (but "kake" appeared in "a hill see a tall river" as well, so "kake" might be "tall")
- "logipo" = birds (plural), "logipa" = the bird (definite)

Let me test more adjectives, other verbs, and how definiteness works with numbers.

PROBES:
1. a big king chase a bird
2. the big king chase a bird
3. a king chase two birds
4. two kings chase a bird
action.label: response

probes: a big king chase a bird
the big king chase a bird
a king chase two birds
two kings chase a bird

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a big king chase a bird"  =>  kafe tuli logi fugo
2. "the big king chase a bird"  =>  kafe tuli logipa fugo
3. "a king chase two birds"  =>  kafepo te tuli logi
4. "two kings chase a bird"  =>  kafe tuli logipo te
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 5 done. Probes used: 12 of 20. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the results:
- "fugo" = big (adjective)
- "tuli" = chase (verb)
- "logipo" = birds (plural), "logipa" = the bird (definite singular)
- "kafe" = king
- "kake" = tall (from "a hill see a tall river")
- Word order: Subject + verb + object
- "kafe kake" = king (with adjective "tall")
- "kafe kake logi fugo" = big king chase bird (adjective on subject)
- "kafe tuli logipa fugo" = the big king chase bird (definite object)
- "kafepo te tuli logi" = two kings chase birds (plural subject, "te" on subject)
- "kafe tuli logipo te" = two kings chase birds (plural object, "te" on object)

So "te" appears to be a plural marker that can go on either subject or object. Let me test more to understand the system better - other adjectives, other verbs, and how they combine.

PROBES:
1. a red king chase a bird
2. a king chase a red bird
3. a cold king chase a bird
4. a king chase a cold bird
action.label: response

probes: a red king chase a bird
a king chase a red bird
a cold king chase a bird
a king chase a cold bird

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a red king chase a bird"  =>  kafe tuli logi fago
2. "a king chase a red bird"  =>  kafe fago tuli logi
3. "a cold king chase a bird"  =>  kafe tuli logi gugefe
4. "a king chase a cold bird"  =>  kafe gugefe tuli logi
STATUS: investigation round 4 of 5 done. Probes used: 16 of 20. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the results:
- "fago" = red (adjective)
- "gugefe" = cold (adjective)
- Adjective position: can go before or after the noun it modifies
- "kafe fago tuli logi" = king red chase bird (adjective before noun)
- "kafe fago tuli logi" vs "kafe tuli logi fago" - different word orders for same meaning
- "kafe gugefe tuli logi" = king cold chase bird (adjective before noun)
- "kafe tuli logi gugefe" = king chase bird cold (adjective after noun)

So adjectives can appear in different positions. Let me test a few more patterns to understand the system better before the exam.

PROBES:
1. a red king chase a red bird
2. a king chase a red red bird
3. a king chase a red bird red
4. two red kings chase a bird
action.label: response

probes: a red king chase a red bird
a king chase a red red bird
a king chase a red bird red
two red kings chase a bird

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a red king chase a red bird"  =>  kafe fago tuli logi fago
2. "a king chase a red red bird"  =>  kafe fago fago tuli logi
3. "a king chase a red bird red"  =>  MALFORMED: unexpected word 'red' in the object
4. "two red kings chase a bird"  =>  kafe tuli logipo fago te

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. the river hear six tall lakes
2. a farmer feed a king
3. two dark birds watch the two red hunters
4. three dogs chase five big farmers
5. five birds find the three hunters
6. five ships chase six stones
7. a river watch the two spears
8. the four red trees hear six spears
9. the five dogs feed five cold spears
10. the three roads watch two hills

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Let me analyze the patterns from my investigation:

1. Word order: Subject + verb + object
2. Articles: "the" = definite (adds -pa to object noun), "a" = indefinite (no article)
3. Numbers: "two" = "te" (plural marker), "three" = "pi", "four" = "se", "five" = "pufa", "six" = "giso"
4. Adjectives can appear before or after the noun
5. Verbs: see=kake, chase=tuli, feed=fugo, find=kutofo, watch=goletopi, hold=gile, carry=fogupo, hear=fosu

Now translating:
1. "the river hear six tall lakes" - river (kugi) definite, hear (fosu), six (giso), tall (kake), lakes (lakes = kugi? but "lakes" plural, so "kugipa"? No, "lake" is "kugi", plural "kugipa")
2. "a farmer feed a king" - farmer (ti), feed (fugo), king (kafe)
3. "two dark birds watch the two red hunters" - two birds (fapo te), dark (lulo), watch (goletopi), the two red hunters (the hunters = "gisapopa" from "five hunters watch" = gisapopa, red = fago, two = te)
4. "three dogs chase five big farmers" - three dogs (lesa pi), chase (tuli), five big farmers (pufa fugo ti)
5. "five birds find the three hunters" - five birds (fapo pufa), find (kutofo), the three hunters (gisapopa pi)
6. "five ships chase six stones" - five ships (gugefe pufa), chase (tuli), six stones (palepo giso)
7. "a river watch the two spears" - river (kugi), watch (goletopi), the two spears (sukupo te)
8. "the four red trees hear six spears" - the four red trees (kutofo fago pi), hear (fosu), six spears (sukupo giso)
9. "the five dogs feed five cold spears" - the five dogs (lesa pufa), feed (fugo), five cold spears (sukupo gugefe pufa)
10. "the three roads watch two hills" - the three roads (tigopo pi), watch (goletopi), two hills (fapopo te)

ANSWERS:
1. kugi fosu giso kake kugipa
2. ti fugo kafe
3. fapo te lulo goletopi gisapopa te fago
4. lesa pi tuli pufa fugo ti
5. fapo pufa kutofo gisapopa pi
6. gugefe pufa tuli palepo giso
7. kugi goletopi sukupo te
8. kutofo fago pi fosu sukupo giso
9. lesa pufa fugo sukupo gugefe pufa
10. tigopo pi goletopi fapopo te
action.label: response

answers: fapo te lulo goletopi gisapopa te fago
lesa pi tuli pufa fugo ti
kutofo fago pi fosu sukupo giso
lesa pufa fugo sukupo gugefe pufa
targets: sukupopa fago te kutopi kafepo lulo te
gugepo fugo ti tuli fapopo pi
kasefupo pufa fosu palepopa fago se
kasefupo gugefe ti totu fapopopa ti
